abstract:
This study examines the evolution of coalition governance in Slovakia from 2008 to 2021, a period marked by significant shifts in the party system and the institutionalizing of executive norms within an unstable political environment. Analyzing the transition from traditional left-right bloc competition to a more fragmented landscape dominated by anti-establishment and populist actors, the research highlights a "gradual settlement of rules" that aligns with the Ministerial Government Model. Under this framework, individual ministers and their respective parties exercise high levels of autonomy, facilitated by the abandonment of cross-party watchdog mechanisms and a reliance on the Coalition Council for conflict resolution. 

The findings suggest that while executive bargaining remains swift and office-oriented, the stability of these governance patterns was severely tested by a series of exogenous shocks, including the 2018 assassination of journalist Ján Kuciak and the subsequent COVID-19 pandemic. Specifically, the pandemic era under Igor Matovič triggered a temporary shift toward a Dominant Prime Minister Model, characterized by personalized crisis management and the bypassing of established institutional protocols. Ultimately, the paper argues that while Slovakia has avoided significant democratic backsliding, the lack of robust mutual control mechanisms within coalitions has historically facilitated state capture and systemic corruption. The study concludes that the personalization of politics and the organizational weakness of newer parties continue to challenge the long-term predictability of Slovak coalition governance.
